overview.title: El viaje a Sevilla
overview.topic: Winning a scholarship and traveling to Seville for the first time

notes.heading: Sequence of tenses: past main verb → imperfect subjunctive
notes.body: When the main clause verb is in the preterite or imperfect and it triggers the subjunctive (via esperar que, pedir que, querer que, etc.), the subordinate clause must use the imperfect subjunctive — not the present subjunctive. Using present subjunctive after a past main verb is a common learner error that sounds unnatural to native speakers. The rule: past tense → imperfect subjunctive; present/future tense → present subjunctive.

notes.heading: Pluperfect (pluscuamperfecto) for past-before-past
notes.body: Había + past participle expresses an action completed before another past action. In 'me dijo que había conseguido una beca', the winning happened before the telling. The pluperfect is the Spanish equivalent of English 'had done'. It appears frequently alongside preterite narration to establish that one event preceded another.

notes.heading: Negative imaginar que + subjunctive vs. affirmative + indicative
notes.body: 'Imaginaba que...' (affirmative) takes the indicative because you are asserting something as fact in your mind. 'No imaginaba que...' (negative) introduces uncertainty — you are saying the fact was outside your mental frame — which triggers the subjunctive. The negation of a verb of thinking or believing typically shifts the subordinate clause from indicative to subjunctive.

notes.heading: Cuando + preterite for the moment of arrival
notes.body: Cuando followed by a preterite describes the specific moment something happened (when the plane landed). Cuando followed by an imperfect describes the general time period when something was true (when I was young). This distinction — cuando + preterite for a moment, cuando + imperfect for a period — is a reliable rule for choosing between the two tenses after temporal conjunctions.

sentences.translation: When I was little, I always hoped that someday I would go to Spain.
rationale: era → imperfecto: sets the habitual past backdrop — an ongoing state of being young.
esperaba → imperfecto: hoping was a continuous, habitual feeling during childhood, not a single event.
fuera → subjuntivo imperfecto: after esperar que, the subordinate clause requires the imperfect subjunctive. The trip was uncertain and desired, not a fact.

sentences.translation: At last, my teacher told me that I had gotten a scholarship to study in Seville.
parts: una beca para estudiar en Sevilla.
rationale: dijo → pretérito: a specific, completed statement at one moment.
había conseguido → pretérito perfecto compuesto (pluscuamperfecto): the winning of the scholarship happened before the telling — an action completed prior to another past action.

sentences.translation: My parents asked that I be very careful during the trip.
rationale: pidieron → pretérito: asking was a single, completed act — they asked me once at a specific moment.
tuviera → subjuntivo imperfecto: pedir que triggers the subjunctive. The main verb is in the preterite, so the subordinate clause uses imperfect subjunctive (sequence of tenses rule).

sentences.translation: When the plane landed in Seville, the sun was shining and the city was beautiful.
rationale: aterrizó → pretérito: landing was a punctual, completed event.
brillaba → imperfecto: the sun shining is an ongoing background condition — part of the scene I arrived into.
era → imperfecto: describes an inherent quality of the city as I perceived it — a descriptive state, not a change.

sentences.translation: I didn't imagine that flamenco would move me so much until I saw my first performance.
rationale: imaginaba → imperfecto: not imagining was a continuous prior state — an ongoing assumption before the show.
emocionara → subjuntivo imperfecto: imaginar que in a negative context introduces doubt/uncertainty, triggering the subjunctive. The emotion was not yet a known fact.
vi → pretérito: seeing the performance was a single, completed event that changed everything.
